segments.text: Hi there, my name is Holt Skinner and
today we're going to explore Notebook
LM, Google's simple yet powerful tool
for information understanding and
organization built from the ground up
with Gemini. We'll also explore Google
Agent Space, an enterprise tool from
Google Cloud which includes Notebook LM
for enterprise. First of all, what is
notebook LM and why is it called that?
Well, think about the physical notebooks
you might use every day. They're a
convenient place to keep track of
information and documents. You can
notate your thoughts, ideas, reminders,
tasks, and other things related to it.
You can keep an organizational system,
or it might be more chaotic, depending
on what works best for you. Now, imagine
if that notebook could understand
everything in it, answer any question
about it, or even help you brainstorm
new ideas. Well, that's what Notebook LM
can do. It's a notebook built with
Gemini, a generative AI language model
or LM, hence notebook
LM. Genai models are great for reading
large amounts of unstructured data and
then writing summaries or answering
specific questions about that data.
Okay, great. So, what can I do with this
and how can I use it? Well, let's jump
right in and show you. All you need is a
Google account. For this example, let's
pretend that we're a student currently
enrolled in a class focused on the
history of computer science, and we're
going to use Notebook LM to help us
study for an exam. Go to
notebooklm.google and click try Notebook
LM to get started. Accept the terms of
service, then click create new
notebook. At this point, we can start
importing our data. We can load in files
from Google Drive, Docs, websites, audio
files, and even YouTube videos. I'll
load in a Google Doc with class
notes, a PDF containing some class
slides, the Wikipedia article on history
of computer
science, and a YouTube video from Crash
Course Computer Science. Once it
finishes loading, I have lots of options
to look at. First, we can see that this
notebook has automatically generated a
title and an emoji based on what we
added. Very clever. On the left side, I
can view the original sources and view
summaries and key topics. I can click on
any of these topics to get a generated
outline of
them. Let's focus on the chat section
here. I get a summary of all the sources
I've uploaded and I can start asking
questions. For example, what if I want
to learn more about Ada Love Lace? I get
a detailed answer about her and the
impact she made on computing. And notice
all these citations. This response from
Gemini is grounded to the information I
provided. And I can check the original
source. So I don't need to worry about
hallucinations or madeup answers from
the model. Notebook. LM also provides me
with some sample questions I can ask in
case I'm stuck on where to go next. This
chat window retains the context of my
previous messages, so I don't need to
repeat myself.
I can even ask for the responses in a
different language. For example, let's
try asking for an answer in
Spanish. Being able to have a
conversation with my notes is already
incredibly useful, but we can do even
more. Let's say I have a final exam
coming up that covers the entire
timeline. I can go under notes and click
on timeline, and notebook LM will write
out a timeline in chronological order of
every key event listed in the notes. I
can then save this as a source which
will be used as context for future chats
and I can easily refer to it
later. I can also create a study guide
for myself which makes a quiz with an
answer key, some essay questions and a
glossery of key
terms. And of course I can add my own
notes that can be converted to sources
if I want to remember them.
Now, let's say I feel completely
overwhelmed with reading and I need to
head outside, but I still need to keep

covered. I can create an audio overview,
which is a two-host podcast where they
deep dive through my whole notebook. I

generate. In just a few minutes, I have
a fun and engaging way to review all

Way older than computers. Way older. I
